name: Prose: "Learning the Game" (Book Extract) — Sachin Tendulkar

sourceRefs.label: 2022_q page 20 Q73 (Achrekar/Tendulkar both started cricket at age 11, stashed from earlier research, with answer key)
civilserviceaspirants.in - two dedicated MCQ sets (12 questions total), Group 2/2A specific

researchNotes: 1 real past-paper hit (stashed earlier while researching 'The Dying Detective', shared OCR page) plus a rich 12-question web-research set. A first-person memoir extract — Sachin Tendulkar himself is 'the narrator' — recounting his rigorous early cricket training under coach Ramakant Achrekar, with his elder brother Ajit playing a key supporting role throughout.

reading.intro: A first-person extract from Sachin Tendulkar's memoir, recounting how his brother Ajit introduced him (at age 11) to coach Ramakant Achrekar at Shardashram Vidyamandir, and the rigorous daily training that followed — including hardships like having only one set of cricket clothes and the now-famous 'one-rupee coin on the stumps' concentration drill.

qa.question: Who are 'the narrator' and the author of "Learning the Game", and which two cricketers did he admire and try to emulate?
qa.answer: The narrator IS the author — Sachin Tendulkar himself, in this first-person memoir extract. He admired and tried to emulate Sunil Gavaskar and Viv Richards.

qa.question: Which school did young Sachin initially attend, and who suggested he change schools, and to where?
qa.answer: He initially attended New English School. Coach Ramakant Achrekar suggested changing his school — to Shardashram Vidyamandir, where Achrekar himself coached cricket.

qa.question: Real exam: How old was Sachin when he first went to Achrekar's cricket camp, and who introduced him there?
qa.answer: 11 years old (confirmed by a real exam question, which also notes Achrekar himself started playing cricket at the same age). His elder brother Ajit introduced him to Achrekar.

qa.question: What was Achrekar's initial reaction to young Sachin's performance, and how did Ajit get him a second chance?
qa.answer: Achrekar initially suggested Ajit bring the boy back when he was OLDER (i.e., rejecting him at first). Ajit insisted Sachin deserved another chance because he knew Sachin could perform better — Achrekar agreed to watch again secretly (pretending to leave, observing from behind a tree/at a distance), and this time Sachin played confidently and was accepted into the camp.

qa.question: What was the daily training routine like during the summer camp, and what hardships did young Sachin endure?
qa.answer: TWO sessions a day — one in the morning and one in the evening, at Shivaji Park — with school squeezed in between. With only ONE set of cricket clothes, he had to wash and dry them between sessions; his pockets were often wet from the effort/perspiration of the rigorous training.

qa.question: What did Ajit discuss with Sachin during their bus journeys to and from practice, and what was Achrekar's famous concentration drill?
qa.answer: Ajit discussed the NUANCES OF BATTING with Sachin during their bus journeys. Achrekar's well-known drill: placing a one-rupee coin on top of the stumps during the last 15 minutes of net practice — whoever bowled Sachin out kept the coin, but if Sachin batted through unbowled, HE kept the coin — a method designed to build intense concentration and mental discipline.

qa.question: What broader lesson does the story convey about talent and success?
qa.answer: That natural talent alone isn't enough — rigorous discipline, sacrifice (enduring hardship like having only one set of clothes), and mentorship (Achrekar's demanding methods, Ajit's constant support) are what transform raw potential into world-class skill.

qa.question: What is significant about Achrekar first rejecting Sachin, then secretly re-observing him?
qa.answer: It shows a coach testing genuine character and performance under pressure rather than judging on a single first impression — and Ajit's persistence in advocating for his brother highlights how crucial family support was in getting Sachin a fair second chance.

options: One session in the morning only

questions.prompt: What did Ajit discuss with the narrator during their bus journeys?

options: He washed and dried them between sessions
He wore them dirty every day
questions.explanation: He washed and dried them between sessions.

questions.prompt: What was Achrekar's famous training drill to build the narrator's concentration?
options: Placing a one-rupee coin on the stumps during the last 15 minutes of practice
Having him practise alone at night
Rewarding him with sweets for every run scored
questions.explanation: Placing a one-rupee coin on the stumps.

questions.prompt: What did Achrekar suggest for the narrator to improve his cricket, regarding his education?
The broader lesson the story conveys about success is that it requires:
options: Discipline, sacrifice, and mentorship combined with talent
questions.explanation: Discipline, sacrifice, and mentorship combined with talent.
